questions.stem: Light criteria classify effusion as exudative if:

citation.supportingQuote: An exudative effusion is diagnosed if one or more of the following criteria are met: Pleural fluid protein/serum protein ratio of more than 0.5; Pleural fluid LDH/serum LDH ratio of more than 0.6; Pleural fluid LDH is more than two-thirds of the upper limit of the normal serum LDH value

questions.stem: Most common cause of pleural effusion overall?

citation.supportingQuote: heart failure...conditions that alter the hydrostatic or oncotic pressures in the pleural space

questions.stem: Milky pleural fluid with TG >110 mg/dL indicates:

citation.supportingQuote: Pleural fluid triglycerides greater than 110 mg/dL signify a chylothorax. The pleural fluid in such conditions typically has a milky-white appearance.

questions.stem: Parapneumonic effusion requires chest tube drainage when:
questions.correctAnswer: pH <7.2, glucose <60, positive Gram stain, or pus (empyema)

citation.supportingQuote: A pH below 7.2 indicates a complex pleural effusion in the setting of pneumonia, which almost always requires CTT insertion for drainage...Chest tube drainage with antibiotic treatment is warranted in complex parapneumonic effusions or empyema.

questions.stem: Maximum volume to remove during therapeutic thoracentesis?

citation.supportingQuote: limiting fluid extraction to no more than 1500 mL per session is important to avoid the risk of reexpansion pulmonary edema

questions.stem: Bilateral pleural effusions in a CHF patient responding to diuretics. Next step?

citation.supportingQuote: A thoracentesis in the setting of heart failure is recommended only if diuretics fail or the patient is significantly symptomatic.

questions.stem: Pleural fluid cytology is most useful for diagnosing:

citation.supportingQuote: Cytological testing is necessary in suspected cases of malignant pleural effusion. The sensitivity of pleural fluid cytology for diagnosing malignant effusions after the first thoracentesis is approximately 60%. However, the diagnostic yield increases with additional attempts, achieving a success rate of up to 90% after 3 samples are collected on separate days.

questions.stem: Costophrenic angle blunting on CXR requires approximately how much fluid?

citation.supportingQuote: the meniscus sign indicates the presence of a significant amount of pleural fluid, typically exceeding 200 mL and blunting the costophrenic angle...a lateral decubitus view can detect as little as 50 mL of fluid in the pleural space
